sections.plain_summary: Thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS) is a group of conditions caused by compression of nerves, arteries, or veins in the thoracic outlet — the narrow space between the collarbone and first rib. It causes pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness in the neck, shoulder, arm, and hand, and can affect circulation. It is often misdiagnosed and can take years to identify correctly.
sections.detailed_summary: Thoracic outlet syndrome refers to compression of one or more of the structures passing through the thoracic outlet: the brachial plexus (a network of nerves), the subclavian artery, or the subclavian vein. This space can be narrowed by anatomical variations (such as a cervical rib or abnormal fibrous bands), poor posture, muscle hypertrophy, trauma, or repetitive overhead activity. TOS is divided into three main subtypes based on which structure is compressed: neurogenic TOS (nTOS), venous TOS (vTOS), and arterial TOS (aTOS). Neurogenic TOS is by far the most common and is also the hardest to confirm with standard tests. Venous and arterial TOS are rarer but more objectively diagnosable and potentially more dangerous due to clot and embolism risk. Symptoms depend on the subtype but commonly include pain radiating into the arm and hand, numbness and tingling (especially in the ulnar distribution — ring and little fingers), weakness, and in vascular forms, swelling, discolouration, or cold extremities. Diagnosis is clinical and involves provocative physical examination tests, imaging (MRI, CT angiography, Doppler ultrasound), and sometimes nerve conduction studies. Treatment ranges from physiotherapy and postural correction to surgical decompression (first rib resection and scalenectomy) in persistent or vascular cases. Recovery can be prolonged and outcomes vary considerably.

prevalence.summary: TOS is uncommon overall, but neurogenic TOS is likely underdiagnosed due to vague symptoms and no definitive test.
prevalence.estimate: Estimated 1–8 per 1,000 people, though figures vary widely by subtype and diagnostic criteria
prevalence.notes: Neurogenic TOS accounts for roughly 95% of cases and is significantly more common in women aged 20–50. Arterial TOS is rare (~1% of TOS cases). Venous TOS accounts for about 4%. Athletes who perform repetitive overhead movements (swimmers, baseball pitchers, volleyball players) are at higher risk. True prevalence is hard to pin down because neurogenic TOS has no gold-standard diagnostic test.

subgroups.name: Neurogenic TOS (nTOS)
subgroups.description: Compression of the brachial plexus, causing nerve-related symptoms. Accounts for ~95% of all TOS cases.
distinguishing_features: Pain, tingling, and numbness in the arm, hand, and fingers — often in the ulnar (ring and little finger) distribution
Arm fatigue and weakness, especially with overhead activity
No reliable single diagnostic test; diagnosis is largely clinical
Symptoms often worsen with prolonged computer use, driving, or overhead tasks
More common in women aged 20–50

subgroups.name: Venous TOS (vTOS) — Paget-Schroetter syndrome
subgroups.description: Compression or thrombosis of the subclavian vein, often following strenuous arm activity.
distinguishing_features: Sudden arm swelling, heaviness, and bluish discolouration (cyanosis)
Prominent superficial veins visible on the chest and shoulder
Often triggered by unaccustomed vigorous arm effort ('effort thrombosis')
Confirmed by Doppler ultrasound or venography
Requires urgent anticoagulation and often intervention to prevent post-thrombotic syndrome

subgroups.name: Arterial TOS (aTOS)
subgroups.description: Compression or injury to the subclavian artery, the rarest and most dangerous form.
distinguishing_features: Cold, pale, or white hand or fingers; reduced pulse in the arm
Ischaemic pain or finger ulceration in severe cases
Often associated with an anatomical anomaly such as a cervical rib
Risk of arterial aneurysm, thrombosis, or distal embolism
Confirmed by CT angiography or MR angiography; requires surgical management

subgroups.name: Disputed or non-specific TOS
subgroups.description: A controversial category sometimes used when symptoms suggest neurogenic TOS but objective evidence is absent. Accepted by some specialists, rejected by others.
distinguishing_features: Diffuse upper extremity pain and fatigue without clear neurological deficit
Normal nerve conduction studies and imaging
Often overlaps with fibromyalgia, chronic pain syndromes, and central sensitisation
Management is almost exclusively physiotherapy and pain management
Diagnosis requires exclusion of all other causes and remains contested

symptoms.symptom: Pain in the neck, shoulder, or upper arm
symptoms.notes: Often described as aching or heaviness; can radiate from the neck down into the arm. May worsen with overhead activity or prolonged arm use.

symptoms.symptom: Numbness and tingling in the hand and fingers
symptoms.notes: Classically affects the ring and little finger (ulnar distribution) in neurogenic TOS, but any finger distribution is possible. Often worse at night or with sustained arm positions.
Difficulty holding items, gripping, or sustaining overhead tasks. Can be subtle early on.
A hallmark of venous TOS. May be diffuse, affecting the whole arm. Onset can be sudden after exertion.
Bluish (cyanosis) in venous TOS; pale or white in arterial TOS. Redness can occur on arm dependency.
Suggests arterial involvement. Can be associated with Raynaud-like episodes.

symptoms.symptom: Prominent veins on the chest or shoulder
symptoms.notes: Visible collateral veins indicate venous obstruction. A key physical finding in venous TOS.
Can occur due to referred pain from scalene muscles or brachial plexus irritation. Occipital location is common.

symptoms.symptom: Reduced or absent radial pulse with arm elevation
symptoms.notes: Detected clinically during provocative tests (e.g. Adson's, EAST test). Pulse obliteration is only meaningful if it also reproduces symptoms.

symptoms.symptom: Wasting of hand muscles (thenar or hypothenar atrophy)
symptoms.notes: A sign of long-standing nerve compression. Uncommon but indicates more severe neurogenic TOS requiring urgent evaluation.

key_patterns: Symptoms are consistently reproduced or worsened by specific arm positions (elevated, abducted, or externally rotated)
Ulnar nerve distribution tingling (ring and little fingers) pointing to lower brachial plexus compression
Sudden unilateral arm swelling in a young, active person after exertion — suspect venous TOS
Pulse changes with arm position alongside symptom reproduction on provocative manoeuvres
Normal standard nerve conduction studies in neurogenic TOS (routine NCS tests distal nerve segments, not the proximal plexus where compression occurs)
Anatomical anomalies on imaging (cervical rib, elongated C7 transverse process, bony deformity from prior clavicle fracture)

at_home.name: Elevated Arm Stress Test (EAST) / Roos Test
at_home.description: Raise both arms to 90 degrees with elbows bent, palms facing forward (goal-post position). Open and close your fists slowly for 3 minutes. Note if symptoms (tingling, pain, arm dropping, fatigue) develop. Healthy individuals can complete 3 minutes without significant symptoms. Stopping early or reproducing your usual symptoms is a positive result.
at_home.safety_notes: Stop if you feel severe pain or significant numbness. This is a screening tool only — pulse obliteration or symptom reproduction should be formally evaluated. Many people without TOS can also develop some symptoms, so context matters.

at_home.name: Symptom and position diary
at_home.description: Track when symptoms occur in relation to arm and head position, activity, and time of day. Note whether overhead work, sleeping with the arm elevated, carrying bags, or typing triggers or worsens symptoms. This pattern can help distinguish TOS from cervical spine or distal nerve entrapment.
at_home.safety_notes: Diary alone cannot diagnose TOS. Use it to inform your consultation with a specialist.

clinical.name: Provocative physical examination tests
clinical.description: A battery of manoeuvres assessing symptom reproduction and pulse changes: Adson's test (head rotation with inspiration), Wright's test (arm hyperabduction), EAST/Roos test, and the Upper Limb Tension Test. No single test is fully reliable; a cluster of positive findings alongside clinical history is most meaningful.
clinical.availability: Performed by a vascular surgeon, neurologist, or physiotherapist experienced in TOS. Requires referral.

clinical.name: Doppler ultrasound
clinical.description: Assesses blood flow in the subclavian and axillary vessels in multiple arm positions. Key test for venous TOS (looking for thrombosis) and arterial TOS (stenosis, aneurysm, embolism). Less useful for neurogenic TOS.
clinical.availability: Available through vascular surgery or radiology referral. Widely available in secondary care.

clinical.name: CT angiography or MR angiography
clinical.description: Cross-sectional imaging of the thoracic outlet vasculature. Identifies bony anomalies, arterial stenosis, aneurysm, and venous thrombosis. Best test for arterial TOS and for surgical planning.
clinical.availability: Ordered by vascular surgeons or radiologists. Available in most secondary care hospitals.

clinical.name: MRI of brachial plexus
clinical.description: High-resolution MRI can visualise brachial plexus anatomy and identify fibrous bands, scarring, or compression. Useful in neurogenic TOS when other causes need to be excluded. Requires dedicated neuroimaging protocol — standard MRI neck may miss relevant findings.
clinical.availability: Specialist MRI brachial plexus protocols available at major centres. Requires referral to neurology or vascular surgery.

clinical.name: Nerve conduction studies (NCS) and electromyography (EMG)
clinical.description: Tests the speed and strength of electrical signals through peripheral nerves and muscles. Standard NCS is often normal in neurogenic TOS because it primarily tests distal nerve segments — not the proximal plexus. Abnormal ulnar sensory amplitude can be a clue. EMG may show denervation in severe or chronic nTOS. Valuable mainly for ruling out other diagnoses (carpal tunnel syndrome, cubital tunnel syndrome, cervical radiculopathy).
clinical.availability: Ordered by neurologists. Available in most secondary care settings.

clinical.name: Cervical spine X-ray and CT
clinical.description: Detects cervical ribs (present in 0.5–1% of the population), elongated C7 transverse processes, and other bony anomalies that predispose to TOS. A standard early investigation.
clinical.availability: Available through GP or specialist referral. Standard radiology.

clinical.name: Scalene muscle block (diagnostic injection)
clinical.description: Injection of local anaesthetic into the anterior scalene muscle under ultrasound guidance. Temporary relief of symptoms following the block supports a diagnosis of neurogenic TOS and may predict response to surgical scalenectomy. Not universally available or accepted.
clinical.availability: Performed at specialist TOS centres by experienced interventional radiologists or surgeons. Not widely available.

related_conditions.name: Cervical radiculopathy
related_conditions.explanation: Compression of nerve roots in the cervical spine causes arm pain, tingling, and weakness in patterns that closely mimic neurogenic TOS. Distinguishing the two requires careful examination and imaging — both can coexist ('double crush' phenomenon).

related_conditions.name: Carpal tunnel syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Median nerve compression at the wrist causes hand tingling and weakness. Can be confused with TOS, and the two can coexist. TOS more commonly affects the ulnar side; carpal tunnel the thumb, index, and middle fingers.

related_conditions.name: Cubital tunnel syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Ulnar nerve compression at the elbow produces ring and little finger tingling identical to the most common pattern in neurogenic TOS. Nerve conduction studies help locate the level of compression.

related_conditions.name: Cervical rib
related_conditions.explanation: A congenital extra rib from C7 vertebra is the most common anatomical cause of TOS, particularly arterial and neurogenic subtypes. Present in roughly 0.5–1% of the population but only a minority develop TOS.

related_conditions.name: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the upper limb
related_conditions.explanation: Venous TOS (Paget-Schroetter syndrome) directly causes upper extremity DVT. This requires urgent anticoagulation and further management to prevent post-thrombotic syndrome and pulmonary embolism.

related_conditions.name: Subclavian artery aneurysm
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic arterial compression in arterial TOS can lead to post-stenotic dilatation and aneurysm formation, with risk of thrombosis and distal embolism to the hand.

related_conditions.name: Raynaud's phenomenon
related_conditions.explanation: Episodes of cold, white, then blue fingers triggered by cold or stress. Can coexist with arterial TOS or be confused with it. TOS-related ischaemia tends to be sustained rather than episodic.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and diffuse upper extremity symptoms can overlap with or be confused with disputed neurogenic TOS. Both involve chronic pain amplification and can coexist.

related_conditions.name: Hypermobile EDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue hypermobility can contribute to joint instability in the shoulder girdle and cervical spine, increasing susceptibility to nerve and vessel compression in the thoracic outlet.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: POTS and TOS co-occur more often than expected, particularly in the context of hypermobile connective tissue disorders. Both can cause upper extremity symptoms and may share structural predispositions.

related_conditions.name: Shoulder impingement syndrome / rotator cuff pathology
related_conditions.explanation: Both cause shoulder and arm pain aggravated by overhead activity. Thorough shoulder examination and targeted imaging help distinguish the two; they can coexist.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic TOS, especially the disputed neurogenic subtype, can drive central sensitisation, amplifying pain beyond the area of physical compression and complicating recovery.

strategies.name: Physiotherapy — postural correction and scalene stretching
strategies.description: The first-line treatment for neurogenic TOS. A physiotherapist experienced in TOS works on correcting forward head posture and rounded shoulders, stretching tight scalene and pectoralis minor muscles, strengthening periscapular and thoracic stabilisers, and improving rib cage mobility. This directly addresses the mechanical compression at the root of most neurogenic TOS.
strategies.notes: Requires a physiotherapist with specific TOS experience — generic shoulder or neck physio may not be effective or can worsen symptoms. Improvement is gradual, typically over 3–6 months of consistent work.

strategies.name: Postural and ergonomic modification
strategies.description: Adjusting workstation setup to reduce forward head posture, raising monitor height, using a supportive chair with armrests, avoiding prolonged overhead positions, and modifying activities that consistently provoke symptoms. Reduces mechanical load on the thoracic outlet during daily life.
strategies.notes: Changes must be sustained — temporary improvement often reverses if the person returns to the same postures. Sleep position matters too; sleeping with the arm overhead commonly worsens symptoms.

strategies.name: NSAIDs or analgesics for pain control
strategies.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g. ibuprofen, naproxen) or simple analgesics (paracetamol) can reduce acute pain and allow participation in physiotherapy. They address symptoms rather than the underlying cause.
strategies.notes: Not a long-term solution. Regular use of NSAIDs carries gastrointestinal, cardiovascular, and renal risks. Discuss with a doctor before prolonged use.

strategies.name: Neuropathic pain medications
strategies.description: Gabapentin, pregabalin, or low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (e.g. amitriptyline) can reduce nerve pain, tingling, and sleep disruption in neurogenic TOS that has not responded to physiotherapy alone.
strategies.notes: These do not address the compression but can improve function and quality of life. Side effects include sedation, dizziness, and weight gain. Require prescription and medical oversight.

strategies.name: Scalene muscle botulinum toxin injection
strategies.description: Injection of botulinum toxin into the anterior scalene muscle temporarily relaxes it, reducing compression and providing a diagnostic and therapeutic window. Can allow more effective physiotherapy.
strategies.notes: Effect is temporary (typically 3–4 months). Requires ultrasound guidance by an experienced practitioner. Available mainly at specialist TOS centres.

strategies.name: Anticoagulation therapy
strategies.description: Mandatory for venous TOS with confirmed thrombosis. Typically low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) bridging to warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC). Catheter-directed thrombolysis may be used in acute cases to clear clot before surgical decompression.
strategies.notes: Duration and type determined by the treating vascular surgeon. Do not delay if venous TOS is suspected — upper limb DVT carries a risk of pulmonary embolism.

strategies.name: Surgical decompression (first rib resection ± scalenectomy)
strategies.description: Removal of the first rib and/or scalene muscles through a transaxillary or supraclavicular approach. Considered when: (1) neurogenic TOS fails to respond to 3–6 months of physiotherapy; (2) venous TOS with underlying structural cause; (3) arterial TOS (almost always requires surgery). Addresses the structural cause of compression.
strategies.notes: Outcomes are best in vascular TOS. Results in neurogenic TOS are variable — success rates of 70–90% are reported but patient selection matters greatly. Complications include pneumothorax, brachial plexus injury, and vascular injury. Should only be undertaken at experienced centres.

strategies.name: Activity modification during flares
strategies.description: Temporarily reducing overhead activity, heavy lifting, and repetitive arm movements during symptom flares prevents further irritation. Identifying personal symptom triggers through a diary helps plan activity pacing.
strategies.notes: Avoidance should be temporary and strategic, not permanent — prolonged immobility and deconditioning worsens TOS outcomes.

things_to_avoid: Carrying heavy bags over the affected shoulder — the downward strap load directly compresses the thoracic outlet
Sleeping with the arm raised above the head or under the pillow — narrows the thoracic outlet and commonly causes night symptoms
Prolonged forward head posture (e.g. looking at a low screen or phone) — increases scalene tension and reduces outlet space
High-impact overhead sports without physiotherapy-guided conditioning — can precipitate or worsen compression
Ignoring signs of venous TOS (sudden arm swelling, cyanosis) — upper limb DVT requires urgent medical assessment, not watchful waiting
Accepting a diagnosis of neurogenic TOS without ruling out cervical disc disease, carpal tunnel, and shoulder pathology — misattribution delays effective treatment
Pursuing surgery for neurogenic TOS without a dedicated trial of expert physiotherapy first — surgery in insufficiently selected patients has poor outcomes

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Neurogenic TOS cannot be confirmed with a blood test or scan. A significant proportion of diagnoses rest on clinical judgement, and both over-diagnosis and under-diagnosis are common.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients in real pain need a concrete diagnosis. Accepting diagnostic uncertainty feels like being told their suffering is not real. Doctors may over-diagnose to provide an actionable label or miss it entirely.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who accept the diagnostic uncertainty and focus on what can be improved — posture, muscle balance, ergonomics, pain management — make more consistent progress than those who pursue an ever-more-definitive diagnosis before engaging with treatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Surgery does not guarantee relief for neurogenic TOS. Reported success rates vary enormously and are lower in unselected populations than in published surgical series.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After months or years of pain, surgery represents hope of a definitive fix. The idea that a structural problem requires a structural solution is intuitive but does not always hold for complex nerve compression syndromes.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who complete a serious, expert-guided physiotherapy programme before surgery, and who have objective findings supporting the diagnosis, have meaningfully better surgical outcomes than those who proceed to surgery early or based on symptoms alone.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Posture and ergonomic habits are the primary driver of neurogenic TOS in most people, and unless these change permanently, symptoms will recur regardless of other treatments.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Changing long-established habits requires sustained effort. Passive treatments (injections, surgery) feel more appealing than the long work of postural retraining.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who rebuild their movement habits over 6–12 months — often working with a physiotherapist and making ergonomic changes at work — achieve lasting symptom reduction that people who rely on passive interventions alone do not.

key_levers.lever: Expert physiotherapy targeting thoracic outlet mechanics
key_levers.why_it_matters: Neurogenic TOS in the majority of cases is driven by muscular imbalance and postural compression that is reversible with targeted rehabilitation. This is the only treatment that addresses the root mechanism non-surgically.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask your GP or specialist for a referral specifically to a physiotherapist with TOS experience. Not all musculoskeletal physiotherapists have this. Look for someone who performs scalene and pectoralis minor stretching, rib mobilisation, and postural neuromuscular retraining.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful improvement typically takes 3–6 months of consistent work; full rehabilitation may take 12 months.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Hours spent each day in compression-provoking positions (forward head, elevated shoulders, rounded back) outweigh anything done in a 30-minute physio session. Workstation and habit changes provide sustained mechanical benefit.
key_levers.how_to_start: Raise your screen to eye level. Set a timer to change position every 30 minutes. Avoid carrying shoulder bags on the affected side. Identify your top two or three daily compression triggers from your symptom diary and address those first.
key_levers.timeline: Benefits from ergonomic changes can be felt within 2–4 weeks if implemented consistently.

key_levers.lever: Early, correct identification of vascular TOS
key_levers.why_it_matters: Venous and arterial TOS require medical and often surgical treatment. Delays in identifying upper limb DVT or arterial compromise lead to preventable complications including post-thrombotic syndrome, limb ischaemia, and stroke.
key_levers.how_to_start: If arm swelling, cyanosis, or reduced pulse develop, attend an emergency department or urgent vascular clinic immediately. Do not attempt to manage vascular TOS with physiotherapy.
key_levers.timeline: Acute vascular TOS requires treatment within hours to days.

key_levers.lever: Addressing central sensitisation and pain amplification
key_levers.why_it_matters: In longstanding or inadequately treated TOS, pain systems become sensitised, meaning the nervous system amplifies pain signals independently of ongoing physical compression. Without addressing this, treatment of the local compression alone does not resolve pain.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss pain neuroscience education with your physiotherapist or a pain psychologist. Consider whether cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), graded activity, or low-dose neuropathic medication is indicated.
key_levers.timeline: Pain reconditioning typically takes months of consistent engagement.

common_mistakes: Starting physiotherapy without a practitioner experienced in TOS — generic neck or shoulder programmes may be unhelpful or worsen symptoms
Pursuing surgery for neurogenic TOS before completing 3–6 months of dedicated, expert physiotherapy
Treating the arm and hand symptoms without addressing the neck and thoracic spine posture that is driving compression
Stopping physiotherapy as soon as symptoms improve rather than completing the full rehabilitation programme — relapse is common with early discontinuation
Missing a concurrent diagnosis (cervical disc herniation, carpal tunnel) that is contributing to symptoms, leading to incomplete treatment
Assuming that because scans are 'normal', the condition does not exist or does not need treatment — neurogenic TOS is primarily a clinical diagnosis
Relying solely on pain medication without engaging with the mechanical root cause

specialists.role: Primary specialist for all subtypes of TOS. Evaluates vascular involvement, orders appropriate imaging, and performs surgical decompression when indicated. The most important specialist for venous and arterial TOS.
specialists.notes: TOS is a relatively rare diagnosis — seek a vascular surgeon who explicitly lists TOS as an area of subspecialty interest. Not all vascular surgeons have significant TOS experience.

specialists.role: Delivers the primary non-surgical treatment for neurogenic TOS. Performs postural assessment, scalene and pectoralis stretching, rib cage mobilisation, and neuromuscular retraining.
specialists.notes: Critical to find a physiotherapist with specific TOS experience. Ask directly whether they have treated TOS patients before and what their treatment approach involves.

specialists.role: Performs and interprets nerve conduction studies and EMG; helps exclude cervical radiculopathy, brachial plexitis, and other neurological mimics. Essential for confirming or ruling out neurogenic TOS.
specialists.notes: Standard NCS is often normal in neurogenic TOS; a neurologist familiar with TOS will interpret results in the full clinical context rather than dismissing the diagnosis based on normal routine studies.

specialists.role: Performs first rib resection via transaxillary or supraclavicular approach. In some centres, a thoracic rather than vascular surgeon performs the decompression procedure.
specialists.notes: Operative outcomes are strongly centre- and surgeon-dependent. Consider seeking a second opinion before proceeding to surgery for neurogenic TOS.

specialists.role: Manages complex chronic pain in longstanding TOS, including neuropathic pain, central sensitisation, and overuse of analgesia. Can prescribe and supervise neuropathic medications.
specialists.notes: Particularly valuable when TOS pain has become chronic and disproportionate to mechanical findings.

specialists.role: Performs ultrasound-guided scalene botulinum toxin injections and, in venous TOS, catheter-directed thrombolysis.
specialists.notes: Scalene botox for TOS is only available at specialist centres.

patient_communities.name: r/thoracicoutlet
patient_communities.notes: Active community of TOS patients sharing diagnostic journeys, surgical experiences, physiotherapy approaches, and specialist recommendations. Valuable for realistic patient-perspective information on what to expect from surgery and conservative management.

patient_communities.name: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Several active Facebook groups exist for TOS patients. Search 'Thoracic Outlet Syndrome Support' on Facebook. Groups often contain experience-sharing on specific surgeons, physiotherapy protocols, and recovery timelines.

patient_communities.name: VASCULAR.org patient resources
patient_communities.notes: Patient-facing information from the Society for Vascular Surgery. Reliable, evidence-based overview of all three TOS subtypes.

learning_resources.title: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome — Society for Vascular Surgery Patient Information
learning_resources.description: Clear, accurate overview of all three TOS subtypes covering symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options. A reliable starting point for newly diagnosed patients.

learning_resources.title: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome: A Comprehensive Review
learning_resources.author_or_source: Kuznetsov MR et al. — various review articles available in PubMed
learning_resources.description: PubMed search for systematic reviews and comprehensive reviews on TOS provides access to current clinical evidence on diagnosis and treatment. Suitable for patients wanting to understand the medical evidence base.

learning_resources.title: Physiotutors: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
learning_resources.description: Physiotutors produces high-quality clinical education videos. Their TOS content covers anatomy, provocative tests, and physiotherapy approaches. Useful for understanding what a physiotherapy assessment should include.

learning_resources.title: r/thoracicoutlet wiki and top posts
learning_resources.description: Patient-curated resources, surgeon recommendations, recovery timelines, and physiotherapy protocols shared by hundreds of TOS patients. The diagnostic and surgical experience content is particularly valuable for those navigating the healthcare system.

learning_resources.title: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome chapter — UpToDate
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, regularly updated clinical summaries on neurogenic, venous, and arterial TOS covering pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management. Requires subscription but may be accessible through a hospital or GP library portal. Best for patients who want to read what their doctors read.

sections.relevance_signal: **Arm or hand goes numb, tingly, or cold** when raising the arm or carrying weight — often with neck or shoulder pain.
